text: Reducing intrathoracic esophageal anastomotic leaks and strictures with a novel anastomotic technique. Anastomotic complications represent a significant source of morbidity and occasional mortality after esophagectomy. Since two thousand nine we have used a novel side to side staple line on staple line technique for now over three hundred intrathoracic esophagogastric anastomosis. This technique was designed to create a wide diameter esophagogastric anastomosis while preserving stomach conduit blood supply with the aim of minimizing the risk of strictures and leaks. In this video, we describe the technique used in our institution's experience.

The thoracic portion of the Ivor Lewis esophagectomy is underway, with a thoracoscope inserted through a chest tube incision for video recording. The patient's spine is positioned towards the left of the screen, and the lung is retracted anteriorly to the right. The gastric conduit is visible in the foreground, with its lesser curve staple line facing laterally. A uniform diameter conduit is created using a 100mm stapler with a green cartridge, delivering two rows of staples with a 4.8mm height. The gastric conduit is delivered into the chest, and the esophagus is mobilized to the conduit tip. The esophagogastric anastomosis begins with the mid-left lateral aspect of the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus aligned over the lesser curve staple line, secured with four tacking sutures placed approximately three centimeters apart.

The surgeon partially transects the esophagus one centimeter distal to the inferior set of tacking sutures. A two-centimeter gastrotomy is created in the gastric conduit across the lesser curve staple line. Five tacking sutures are placed between the adjacent conduit and esophageal walls, with the first suture positioned in the middle through the lesser curve staple line and two additional sutures on either side.

The surgeon performs the stapled portion of the esophagogastric anastomosis using a linear endoscopic stapler with a 45mm green cartridge, creating staples with a 4.1mm height. The narrow anvil is positioned within the esophageal lumen, while the large reload jaw is placed within the conduit lumen. After firing the stapler, the previous lesser curve staple line is restapled and cut, forming a side-to-side communication. The esophageal transection is completed sharply, and a silk suture is placed at the center of the esophageal opening for retraction. An ellipse of conduit is removed over the lesser curve staple line to preserve blood supply.

The surgeon closes the open common lumen using two layers of sutures. The inner layer consists of inverted, interrupted 3-0 polyglyctin sutures, tied after placement. The outer layer uses interrupted 3-0 silk sutures with a limbered technique, starting at both corners. A modified horizontal mattress suture is placed at the junction of the gastric conduit staple line. The orienting sutures are cut, and the completed anastomosis is inspected. Soft tissue coverage is achieved with a pleural flap mobilized over the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus, secured with interrupted 3-0 silk sutures.

The surgeon eliminates any residual redundancy in the gastric conduit by over-sewing the superior aspect of the lesser curve staple line using a limbered technique, extending inferiorly until reaching the right gastric vessels. A nasogastric tube is inserted into the conduit to the level of the cruce before closing the chest. Chest tubes are placed and set to water seal.
